title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer target, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to target. You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

examples.explanation: Because nums[0] + nums[1] == 9, we return [0, 1].
nums[1] + nums[2] == 6, so we return [1, 2].

hints: Think about what information you need to find a complement for each number.
A hash map can help you look up whether a complement exists in constant time.
Iterate through the array once; for each element, check if target - nums[i] is already in your hash map. If yes, return both indices. Otherwise, store the current element and its index.
follow_ups: What if the array is sorted? Can you solve it in O(1) space?
What if there could be multiple valid pairs? Return all of them.
How would you handle the case where the input is a stream of numbers?

rubric.approach: Use a hash map to store each number's index as you iterate. For each element, check whether its complement (target - current) already exists in the map. This gives a single-pass O(n) solution.
edge_cases: Duplicate values in the array (e.g., [3, 3] with target 6)
Only two elements in the array
Large values near integer overflow boundaries
rubric.optimization: The brute-force O(n^2) approach checks all pairs. The optimal approach uses a hash map for O(n) time at the cost of O(n) space. If the array were sorted, a two-pointer approach would give O(1) space.

voice_script.intro: Alright, let's get started. I'm going to give you a classic problem. You're given an array of integers and a target number. I need you to find two numbers in that array that add up to the target and return their indices. You can assume there's exactly one solution, and you can't use the same element twice. The order of the returned indices doesn't matter.

clarifications.Can the array contain negative numbers?: Yes, the array can contain negative numbers.
clarifications.Is the array sorted?: No, the array is not necessarily sorted. Don't make any assumptions about ordering.
clarifications.Can I modify the original array?: I'd prefer you don't modify the input, but let's discuss your approach first and then we can talk about trade-offs.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
description: You are given an array prices where prices[i] is the price of a given stock on the i-th day. You want to maximize your profit by choosing a single day to buy one stock and choosing a different day in the future to sell that stock. Return the maximum profit you can achieve from this transaction. If you cannot achieve any profit, return 0.

examples.explanation: Buy on day 2 (price = 1) and sell on day 5 (price = 6), profit = 6 - 1 = 5. Note that buying on day 2 and selling on day 1 is not allowed because you must buy before you sell.
In this case, no transactions are done and the max profit is 0 because prices only decrease.

constraints: You must buy before you sell.
You may complete at most one transaction.
hints: Think about what you need to track as you scan through the prices from left to right.
At each day, the best profit you can get is the current price minus the lowest price you've seen so far.
Maintain a running minimum price and a running maximum profit. Update both as you iterate through the array in a single pass.
optimal_solution:
def max_profit(prices):
    min_price = float('inf')
    max_profit = 0
    for price in prices:
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
        max_profit = max(max_profit, price - min_price)
    return max_profit
follow_ups: What if you could buy and sell multiple times (unlimited transactions)?
What if you could complete at most two transactions?
What if there is a cooldown period of one day after selling before you can buy again?

rubric.approach: Track the minimum price seen so far and the maximum profit achievable. For each day, update the minimum price, then compute profit as current price minus the minimum. Track the best profit across all days.
edge_cases: Array has only one element (cannot make a transaction)
Maximum profit occurs at the very last day
rubric.optimization: Brute force checks all O(n^2) pairs. The optimal single-pass approach runs in O(n) time and O(1) space by maintaining a running minimum.

voice_script.intro: Here's the scenario. Imagine you have historical stock prices, one price per day, in an array. You get to pick one day to buy and one later day to sell. Your goal is to maximize your profit. If there's no way to make a profit, just return zero. So, given this array of daily prices, what's the best profit you can achieve?

clarifications.Can I buy and sell on the same day?: No, you need to buy on one day and sell on a strictly later day.
clarifications.Can prices be zero?: Yes, a price can be zero. It just means the stock is worthless that day.
clarifications.What if the array is empty?: You can assume the array has at least one element, but think about what happens with just one element.

title: Container With Most Water
description: You are given an integer array height of length n. There are n vertical lines drawn such that the two endpoints of the i-th line are (i, 0) and (i, height[i]). Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container holds the most water. Return the maximum amount of water a container can store. Notice that you may not slant the container.

examples.explanation: The lines at index 1 (height 8) and index 8 (height 7) form the container with the most water. Width = 8 - 1 = 7, height = min(8, 7) = 7, area = 7 * 7 = 49.
Width = 1, height = min(1, 1) = 1, area = 1.

hints: The area between two lines is limited by the shorter line. Think about how to maximize both width and height.
Consider starting with the widest possible container and then trying to find taller lines.
Use two pointers, one at each end. Move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inward, because moving the taller one can only decrease the width without guaranteeing a height increase.
follow_ups: Can you prove why moving the shorter pointer inward is always correct?
How would this change if the container could be slanted?
What if you needed to find the top-k containers by area?

rubric.approach: Use two pointers starting at the leftmost and rightmost lines. Compute the area formed by these two lines. Move the pointer at the shorter line inward, since the area is bottlenecked by the shorter line and reducing width is only worthwhile if we might find a taller line.
edge_cases: All heights are equal (any pair with max width is optimal)
Heights are strictly increasing or decreasing
One or more heights are zero
rubric.optimization: Brute force checks all O(n^2) pairs. The two-pointer greedy approach runs in O(n) by eliminating pairs that provably cannot beat the current best.

voice_script.intro: Alright, picture this: you've got a series of vertical lines on a chart, each at a different height. You want to pick two of these lines and use them as the walls of a container to hold water. The water level is limited by the shorter of the two lines, and the width is the distance between them. Which two lines would you pick to hold the maximum amount of water?

clarifications.Is the water level determined by the sho: Exactly. The water can only go as high as the shorter of the two lines you pick.
clarifications.Do the lines between the two chosen line: No. Think of it as just two walls with nothing in between. Lines in the middle don't block water.
clarifications.Can height values be zero?: Yes, a line can have height zero, which means it doesn't hold any water on that side.

title: 3Sum
description: Given an integer array nums, return all the triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i != j, i != k, and j != k, and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] == 0. Notice that the solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.

examples.explanation: The distinct triplets that sum to zero are [-1, -1, 2] and [-1, 0, 1]. The order of the output and the order of the triplets does not matter.
No triplet sums to zero.
The only possible triplet sums to zero.

constraints: The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.
Each triplet must use three distinct indices.
hints: Sorting the array first makes it much easier to avoid duplicates and enables efficient searching.
After sorting, fix one element and reduce the problem to Two Sum on the remaining subarray.
For each fixed element, use two pointers on the sorted remainder. Skip duplicate values for the fixed element and for the two pointers to avoid duplicate triplets.
follow_ups: How would you generalize this to kSum for an arbitrary k?
What if you needed the triplet closest to a given target instead of exactly zero?
Can you solve this without sorting the array?

rubric.approach: Sort the array. Iterate through each element as a fixed first value. For each, use two pointers (one at start of remaining, one at end) to find pairs that sum to the negative of the fixed value. Skip duplicates at every level to ensure unique triplets.
edge_cases: All elements are positive or all negative (no valid triplet)
Minimum length array of exactly 3 elements
rubric.optimization: Brute force is O(n^3). Sorting + two pointers brings it to O(n^2). The key insight is that sorting enables both efficient two-pointer search and easy duplicate skipping.

voice_script.intro: Okay, here's your problem. You're given an array of integers, and I need you to find all unique triplets in the array that sum to zero. By unique, I mean no duplicate triplets in your result, even if the same values appear multiple times in the input. How would you approach this?

clarifications.Can the same element be used more than o: No, each triplet must use three elements at distinct indices. But if a value appears multiple times in the array, you can use different copies.
clarifications.Does the order of elements in each tripl: No, the order within each triplet doesn't matter. [-1, 0, 1] and [0, -1, 1] are the same triplet.

title: Merge Intervals
description: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i], merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.

examples.explanation: Since intervals [1,3] and [2,6] overlap, they are merged into [1,6].
Intervals [1,4] and [4,5] are considered overlapping since they share endpoint 4.

constraints: The input intervals are not necessarily sorted.
hints: What would make it easier to detect overlapping intervals? Think about ordering.
If you sort intervals by their start time, overlapping intervals will be adjacent.

After sorting, iterate through the intervals. If the current interval's start is less than or equal to the previous merged interval's end, merge them by extending the end. Otherwise, start a new merged interval.
follow_ups: How would you insert a new interval into an already sorted, non-overlapping list of intervals?
What if intervals are arriving as a stream and you need to merge on the fly?
How would you find the intersection of two lists of intervals instead of merging?

rubric.approach: Sort intervals by start time. Initialize a result list with the first interval. For each subsequent interval, check if it overlaps with the last interval in the result (current start <= last end). If so, merge by updating the end to the max of both ends. Otherwise, append as a new interval.
edge_cases: An interval is entirely contained within another (e.g., [1,10] and [3,5])
rubric.optimization: Sorting dominates at O(n log n). The merge step is O(n). Without sorting, you'd need O(n^2) pairwise comparisons. The sort-then-scan pattern is standard for interval problems.

voice_script.intro: Let me give you an interval problem. You've got a collection of intervals, each with a start and end point. Some of these intervals overlap. I need you to merge all the overlapping ones together and return a set of non-overlapping intervals that cover the same range. For example, if you have [1,3] and [2,6], those overlap, so you'd merge them into [1,6].

clarifications.Are the intervals sorted?: No, don't assume they're sorted. They can come in any order.
clarifications.Do touching intervals like [1,4] and [4,: Yes, if one interval ends where another begins, they should be merged.
clarifications.Can start equal end in an interval?: Yes, an interval like [3,3] is valid. It represents a single point.

title: Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. An input string is valid if: open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets, open brackets must be closed in the correct order, and every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

examples.explanation: A single pair of matching parentheses.
Three separate pairs, each correctly matched.
The open parenthesis is closed by a bracket of a different type.

constraints: The string contains no other characters.
hints: Think about what data structure naturally handles matching in a last-in-first-out order.
A stack works well here. Push opening brackets, and when you see a closing bracket, check if the top of the stack matches.
Don't forget to check that the stack is empty at the end. A string like '(' has unmatched opening brackets.
follow_ups: What if the string also contains other characters that should be ignored?
What is the minimum number of insertions needed to make the string valid?
How would you handle a custom set of bracket types, provided as input?

rubric.approach: Use a stack. Iterate through the string. For each opening bracket, push it. For each closing bracket, check that the stack is non-empty and the top matches the expected opening bracket. After processing all characters, verify the stack is empty.
edge_cases: Interleaved brackets like '([)]' which is invalid
rubric.optimization: This is already optimal at O(n) time and O(n) space. The stack size is at most n/2. Early termination when a mismatch is found can help in practice.

voice_script.intro: Here's a quick one. You're given a string that only contains parentheses, square brackets, and curly braces. I need you to determine whether the string is valid. That means every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket of the same type, and they're nested correctly. For example, '()[]{}' is valid, but '(]' is not.

clarifications.Can the string be empty?: Yes, and an empty string is considered valid.
clarifications.Will there be any characters other than: No, the string only contains '(', ')', '{', '}', '[', and ']'.
clarifications.Does the order of different bracket type: Only nesting matters. '({[]})' is valid because everything is properly nested and matched.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string s, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string.

examples.explanation: The longest substring without repeating characters is 'abc', which has length 3.
The longest substring without repeating characters is 'b', with length 1.
The longest substring is 'wke', with length 3. Note that 'pwke' is a subsequence, not a substring.

constraints: s consists of English letters, digits, symbols, and spaces.
hints: Think about using a window that expands and shrinks to track the current substring.
A sliding window with a set or hash map can help you efficiently track which characters are in the current window.
Use a hash map to store the last index of each character. When you encounter a repeat, jump the left boundary of the window past the previous occurrence.
space_complexity: O(min(n, m)) where m is the character set size
follow_ups: What if you needed to return the actual substring, not just its length?
What if you were allowed at most k repeating characters?
How would you solve this for a stream of characters?

rubric.approach: Use a sliding window with two pointers (left and right). Maintain a hash map of each character to its most recent index. Expand the right pointer one character at a time. If the character already exists in the window, move the left pointer past its previous occurrence. Track the maximum window size throughout.
edge_cases: All characters are unique (answer is the full string length)
String with spaces and special characters
rubric.optimization: A naive approach checks all O(n^2) substrings. The sliding window approach achieves O(n) because each character is visited at most twice (once by each pointer). Using a hash map for last-seen index lets you jump the left pointer directly instead of incrementing one by one.

voice_script.intro: Alright, here's your problem. Given a string, find the length of the longest substring that doesn't contain any repeating characters. Keep in mind I'm asking about substrings, which are contiguous, not subsequences. For instance, in 'abcabcbb', the answer is 3 because 'abc' is the longest substring with all unique characters.

clarifications.What kinds of characters can the string: It can have lowercase and uppercase letters, digits, symbols, spaces -- anything really.
clarifications.Is the string case-sensitive?: Yes, 'A' and 'a' are treated as different characters.

title: Group Anagrams
description: Given an array of strings strs, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order. An anagram is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, using all the original letters exactly once.

examples.explanation: The anagram groups are: 'eat','tea','ate' are all anagrams; 'tan','nat' are anagrams; 'bat' has no anagram in the list.
A single empty string forms its own group.

constraints: strs[i] consists of lowercase English letters.
The grouping order does not matter.
hints: Two strings are anagrams if and only if they share some canonical form. What could that canonical form be?
Sorting each string gives a canonical key. All anagrams will have the same sorted form.
Use a hash map where the key is the sorted string and the value is a list of original strings with that key. Alternatively, use a character frequency count tuple as the key.
time_complexity: O(n * k log k) where n is the number of strings and k is the max string length
follow_ups: Can you achieve O(n * k) time by using character counts as keys instead of sorting?
How would you handle Unicode characters or case-insensitive anagrams?
What if the input is a stream of strings arriving one at a time?

rubric.approach: Create a hash map keyed by a canonical representation of each string (either sorted characters or a character frequency tuple). Iterate through the input, compute the key for each string, and append it to the corresponding group in the map. Return all groups.
edge_cases: All strings are anagrams of each other
rubric.optimization: Sorting each string takes O(k log k). Using a character count tuple as a key (26 counters for lowercase English) reduces per-string work to O(k). Both approaches are acceptable in interviews.

voice_script.intro: Here's what I'd like you to do. You're given a list of strings, and I need you to group all the anagrams together. Anagrams are words that contain exactly the same letters, just rearranged. For example, 'eat', 'tea', and 'ate' are all anagrams of each other. You can return the groups in any order.

clarifications.Are all strings lowercase?: Yes, all strings consist of only lowercase English letters.
clarifications.Can the input contain empty strings?: Yes, and empty strings are considered anagrams of each other.
clarifications.Does the order within each group matter?: No, the order within groups and the order of groups in the output don't matter.

title: Minimum Window Substring
description: Given two strings s and t of lengths m and n respectively, return the minimum window substring of s such that every character in t (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string. The answer is guaranteed to be unique if it exists.

examples.explanation: The minimum window substring 'BANC' includes 'A', 'B', and 'C' from string t.
The entire string s is the minimum window.
Both 'a's from t must be included, but s only has one 'a', so no valid window exists.

constraints: s and t consist of uppercase and lowercase English letters.
If a valid window exists, it is unique.
Characters in t may have duplicates that must all be present in the window.
hints: Think about using a sliding window that you expand to include all required characters and then shrink to find the minimum.
Use two hash maps: one for the character counts needed from t, and one for the counts in your current window.

Expand the right pointer until all characters are covered. Then contract the left pointer to find the smallest valid window. Track a 'formed' count for how many unique characters meet the required frequency.
follow_ups: Can you solve it in O(n) time if t has no duplicate characters?
What if you needed to find the minimum window that contains t as a subsequence instead of just the characters?
How would you handle this if s is a stream?

rubric.approach: Use a sliding window with two pointers on s. Count character frequencies needed from t. Expand the right pointer to include characters until all of t's characters are covered at the required frequencies. Then contract from the left to find the minimum valid window. Track the best answer throughout.
edge_cases: t is longer than s (impossible, return empty)
t has duplicate characters requiring multiple occurrences
All characters of s are the same
rubric.optimization: Brute force checking all substrings is O(m^2 * n). The sliding window approach is O(m + n) because each character in s is visited at most twice (once by each pointer). Using a formed counter avoids repeatedly comparing full frequency maps.

voice_script.intro: Alright, this one is a bit more challenging. You have two strings, s and t. I need you to find the smallest substring of s that contains every character in t, including duplicates. So if t is 'ABC', you need a window in s that has at least one A, one B, and one C. If t were 'AAB', you'd need at least two A's and one B. Return the smallest such window, or an empty string if none exists.

clarifications.Can t have duplicate characters?: Yes, and your window must contain at least that many of each character. If t has two A's, your window needs at least two A's.
clarifications.What if multiple windows have the same m: The problem guarantees the answer is unique if it exists, so you don't need to worry about ties.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
description: Given a string s, return the longest palindromic substring in s. A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. If there are multiple answers of the same length, return any one of them.

examples.explanation: "bab" is a palindrome of length 3. "aba" is also a valid answer.
"bb" is the longest palindromic substring.
A single character is always a palindrome.

constraints: s consists of only digits and English letters.
If there are multiple answers of the same maximum length, any one is acceptable.
hints: A palindrome mirrors around its center. How many possible centers are there in a string of length n?
There are 2n - 1 centers: n single-character centers (odd-length palindromes) and n - 1 between-character centers (even-length palindromes).
For each center, expand outward while the characters on both sides match. Track the longest palindrome found.
follow_ups: Can you solve this in O(n) time using Manacher's algorithm?
What if you needed to count the total number of palindromic substrings instead?
How would you find the longest palindromic subsequence (not substring)?

rubric.approach: Use expand-around-center. For each index, treat it as the center of both an odd-length and even-length palindrome. Expand outward while characters match. Track the longest palindrome found across all centers.
edge_cases: All characters are the same (entire string is a palindrome)
No palindrome longer than 1 (all characters distinct)
rubric.optimization: Brute force checks all O(n^2) substrings and verifies each in O(n), giving O(n^3). Expand-around-center is O(n^2) and very interview-friendly. Manacher's algorithm achieves O(n) but is rarely expected in interviews.

voice_script.intro: Let's work on this one. Given a string, I want you to find the longest substring that is a palindrome. Remember, a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards. For example, in the string 'babad', you could return 'bab' or 'aba' -- both are palindromes of length 3 and that's the longest possible.

clarifications.What if there are multiple palindromes o: Just return any one of them. Either is fine.
clarifications.Are single characters considered palindr: Yes, every single character is a palindrome. So the answer is always at least length 1.
clarifications.Is the comparison case-sensitive?: Yes, the string only contains digits and English letters, and case matters.
